Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures the file-integrity tool is present and that a valid baseline integrity database has been generated and installed in its active location so that subsequent integrity checks succeed.

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q "^ii  aide "; then
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y aide
fi

# Initialize AIDE if not already initialized
AIDE_DB="/var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz"
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide/aide.conf"

if [ ! -f "$AIDE_DB" ]; then
    sudo aideinit
    sudo mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz "$AIDE_DB"
fi

# Ensure the AIDE database is up-to-date
sudo aide --check || {
    sudo aide --init
    sudo mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz "$AIDE_DB"
}
```

Each of these audit tool executables must be registered with AIDE for cryptographic verification covering their permissions, ownership, and content hashes, so that any modification, replacement, or tampering of the tools can be detected. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures the audit tools are tracked by the file-integrity tool, with the configuration persisting across reboots.

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the list of audit tool binaries to monitor
AUDIT_TOOLS=(
    "/sbin/auditctl"
    "/sbin/aureport"
    "/sbin/ausearch"
    "/sbin/autrace"
    "/sbin/auditd"
    "/sbin/augenrules"
    "/sbin/aulast"
    "/sbin/aulastlog"
    "/sbin/ausyscall"
    "/sbin/auvirt"
)

# Define the AIDE configuration file
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide/aide.conf"

# Define the AIDE rule for audit tools
AIDE_RULE="AuditTools"

# Function to add a rule to the AIDE configuration if it doesn't exist
add_aide_rule() {
    local rule_name="$1"
    local rule_definition="$2"
    if ! grep -q "^${rule_name} =" "$AIDE_CONF"; then
        echo "${rule_name} = ${rule_definition}" >> "$AIDE_CONF"
    fi
}

# Function to add a file to the AIDE configuration if it doesn't exist
add_aide_file() {
    local file_path="$1"
    local rule_name="$2"
    if ! grep -q "^${file_path}" "$AIDE_CONF"; then
        echo "${file_path} ${rule_name}" >> "$AIDE_CONF"
    fi
}

# Ensure the AIDE rule for audit tools is defined
add_aide_rule "$AIDE_RULE" "p+i+n+u+g+s+m+c+md5+sha1+sha256"

# Ensure each audit tool is tracked by AIDE
for tool in "${AUDIT_TOOLS[@]}"; do
    add_aide_file "$tool" "$AIDE_RULE"
done

# Initialize AIDE database if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f "/var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz" ]; then
    aideinit
    mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
fi

# Ensure AIDE is set to run at boot
if ! grep -q "aide --check" /etc/crontab; then
    echo "@reboot root /usr/bin/aide --check" >> /etc/crontab
fi
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system using the default GNOME graphical desktop environment, configure the desktop so that removing a user's smartcard immediately locks the screen, preventing unauthorized access to an unattended session when a user steps away with their card. The setting must be enforced as a mandatory system-wide policy that ordinary users cannot override, and it must persist across reboots. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that establishes this behavior.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the required package is installed
apt-get update
apt-get install -y libpam-pkcs11

# Create the configuration directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p /etc/pam_pkcs11

# Configure PAM to lock the screen on smartcard removal
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/pam_pkcs11/pam_pkcs11.conf
use_pkcs11_module = opensc {
    module = /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/opensc-pkcs11.so;
    description = "OpenSC PKCS#11 module";
};

use_mappers = digest;

digest {
    module = internal;
    func = digest;
    params = "sha1";
};

EOF

# Configure the PAM service to lock the screen on smartcard removal
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/pam.d/common-auth
auth    [success=2 default=ignore]      pam_pkcs11.so
auth    [success=1 default=ignore]      pam_unix.so nullok_secure
auth    requisite                       pam_deny.so
auth    required                        pam_permit.so
auth    optional                        pam_cap.so
EOF

# Create a script to lock the screen on smartcard removal
cat << 'EOF' > /usr/local/bin/lock-on-smartcard-removal.sh
#!/bin/bash
loginctl lock-session
EOF

chmod +x /usr/local/bin/lock-on-smartcard-removal.sh

# Configure udev rule to trigger the lock script on smartcard removal
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/udev/rules.d/99-smartcard-lock.rules
ACTION=="remove", SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ENV{ID_SMARTCARD_READER}=="1", RUN+="/usr/local/bin/lock-on-smartcard-removal.sh"
EOF

# Reload udev rules
udevadm control --reload-rules
udevadm trigger

# Ensure the script is idempotent by checking if the configuration already exists
if ! grep -q "pam_pkcs11.so" /etc/pam.d/common-auth; then
    echo "PAM configuration for smartcard removal lock is already set."
fi

if [ -f /etc/udev/rules.d/99-smartcard-lock.rules ]; then
    echo "Udev rule for smartcard removal lock is already set."
fi
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system running the GNOME desktop environment, configure the graphical desktop so that the screensaver automatically activates after 15 minutes of user inactivity, and ensure this setting is enforced system-wide and locked so that individual users cannot override it. This prevents an unattended workstation from remaining accessible when a user steps away. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that establishes and locks this inactivity timeout, taking effect persistently.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the dconf database and key for the screensaver timeout
SCHEMA="org.gnome.desktop.session"
KEY="idle-delay"
TIMEOUT=900  # 15 minutes in seconds

# Define the dconf profile and lock directories
PROFILE_DIR="/etc/dconf/profile"
LOCKS_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d/locks"
DB_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d"

# Create the dconf profile if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "$PROFILE_DIR"
echo "user-db:user" > "$PROFILE_DIR/user"
echo "system-db:local" >> "$PROFILE_DIR/user"

# Create the dconf database directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "$DB_DIR"

# Set the screensaver timeout in the dconf database
echo "[$SCHEMA]" > "$DB_DIR/00-screensaver"
echo "$KEY=$TIMEOUT" >> "$DB_DIR/00-screensaver"

# Create the locks directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "$LOCKS_DIR"

# Lock the screensaver timeout setting
echo "/$SCHEMA/$KEY" > "$LOCKS_DIR/00-screensaver-lock"

# Update the dconf database
dconf update
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system that uses the GNOME graphical desktop, configure the GNOME Display Manager so that an approved login warning banner is displayed on the graphical login screen before a user authenticates, satisfying the requirement to present a use-notification message to human users. The banner display must be enabled and locked so that ordinary users cannot disable or override it, and the configuration must persist across reboots. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that achieves this end state.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the banner text
BANNER_TEXT="Authorized users only. Unauthorized access is prohibited."

# Define the path to the GDM configuration file
GDM_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/00-login-banner"

# Define the lock file path
LOCK_FILE="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/locks/login-banner"

# Ensure the dconf directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/locks

# Create or update the GDM configuration file with the banner text
cat <<EOF > "$GDM_CONFIG_FILE"
[org/gnome/login-screen]
banner-message-enable=true
banner-message-text='$BANNER_TEXT'

# Create or update the lock file to prevent users from changing the settings
echo "/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-enable" > "$LOCK_FILE"
echo "/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-text" >> "$LOCK_FILE"

# Update the dconf database
dconf update

# Ensure the gdm service is restarted to apply changes
systemctl restart gdm
```

Specifically, ensure that when free disk space available to the audit logs falls to 25 percent or below remaining capacity, the daemon triggers its low-space notification action. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that enforces this threshold and persists across reboots.

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the threshold for low disk space warning
THRESHOLD=25

# Ensure the auditd service is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q auditd; then
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y auditd
fi

# Create or update the auditd configuration for space_left
AUDITD_CONF="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"
grep -q "^space_left =" "$AUDITD_CONF" && \
    sed -i "s/^space_left =.*/space_left = ${THRESHOLD}%/" "$AUDITD_CONF" || \
    echo "space_left = ${THRESHOLD}%" >> "$AUDITD_CONF"

# Ensure the auditd service is enabled and started
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl restart auditd
```
